title: Recognising and treating pig scours
subtitle: Loose droppings are the early warning sign for a long list of pig problems.
excerpt: Scours are the catch-all term for diarrhoea in pigs and they almost always mean something has gone wrong upstream. This covers the four most common causes on a UK smallholding, what each one looks like, when to treat at home, and when to call the vet.
sourceNotes: AHDB Pork pig health and disease factsheets, scour identification chapter. DEFRA welfare code for pigs. University of Wisconsin Extension small-farm pig health guides.

glossaryTerms.definition: The common smallholder term for diarrhoea in pigs and other livestock. Useful as a description of the symptom rather than the disease, because the underlying causes vary widely.
Loss of body water at a rate the animal cannot replace by drinking. Scouring pigs lose water fast and a piglet under three weeks can be dangerously dehydrated in a few hours.

content.text: Scours

in adult pigs and weaners are the most common symptom that brings a smallholder out at first light to look more closely at one animal. Loose droppings are not in themselves an emergency, but they always tell you something. The colour, smell, and any blood in the droppings narrow down what is wrong; the age of the pig and what changed in the last few days narrow it down further.
What healthy droppings look like

A pig on grass and concentrate feed drops a firm sausage that holds its shape, mid-brown, and breaks cleanly when prodded. The smell is earthy rather than sour. Anything looser than soft-set jam, anything yellow or greenish, anything streaked or watery, is a scour and starts the diagnostic walk-through.
Cause one: feed change

The most common cause on a smallholding is a sudden feed change. A new sack of pig nuts from a different mill, a load of windfall apples in late September, a sudden flush of spring grass after weeks of dry. The gut bacteria take three or four days to adjust and the pig scours in the meantime. Loose, mid-brown, no smell change beyond a faint sour note. The pig is bright, eating, and drinking normally.

The fix is patience. Hold the new feed steady and the scour clears in two to three days. Mix the new sack half-and-half with the old for the first week if you have any of the old left. Soak hard nuts in warm water to take the edge off them. Provide clean water at all times and a handful of dry hay; the fibre slows gut transit and firms the dropping up.
Cause two: worms

A pig that has not been wormed since arriving on the holding, or one on tired ground where the same pigs have run for many seasons, will pick up a parasite burden. Scour is a later symptom; first comes poor coat condition, slower growth, and the pig looking generally rough. Adult worms or worm segments may be visible in the dropping. Worm with a licenced pig wormer at the rate printed on the bottle and the scour clears within a week.
Cause three: bacterial or viral infection

The serious end of the list. E. coli, salmonella, swine dysentery, and rotavirus all cause scour and all need a vet diagnosis. The signal that pushes you from home-treatment territory into vet territory is any of: blood or mucus in the dropping, the pig refusing to eat, fever (the pig feels hot and shivers), or more than one pig in the group going down at the same time. Dehydration sets in fast with these infections; piglets under three weeks can die in under twenty-four hours.

Move the affected pig to a clean dry pen if you can, keep water available, and call the vet. The vet will take a faecal sample, identify the organism, and prescribe targeted treatment. Do not give over-the-counter human medicines to pigs.
Cause four: stress

Weaning, transport, mixing with a new group, a fox raid at night, a sudden weather change. Stress disrupts gut motility and causes a short loose-stool episode that clears once the pig settles. Watch for any of the more serious signs alongside; stress on its own does not bring blood, mucus, or fever.
Watching for dehydration
Any scouring pig is losing water faster than normal.
Dehydration

is the quickest danger. Pinch the skin on the neck of the pig and watch how fast it springs back: a slow snap-back means the pig is short of water. A piglet with sunken eyes and a slack skin tent needs oral rehydration solution at once and a vet call within the hour. An adult sow with the same signs can be helped along with a bucket of warm electrolyte mix while you wait.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Reading the dropping
attrs.intro: Match the symptom to the likely cause before you decide whether to treat or call.

items.symptom: Loose mid-brown drops two days after a new sack of feed was started.
items.fix: Hold the new feed steady. Soak any hard nuts. Add a handful of dry hay. The scour clears in two to three days. Mix old feed half-and-half for the first week of any future feed change.

items.symptom: Rough coat, slow growth, occasional worm segments in dropping.
items.fix: Worm with a licenced pig wormer at the rate on the bottle. Move the pigs to fresh ground if you have it. Repeat the worming in three weeks to catch any new hatch from the larval stage.

items.symptom: Yellow watery dropping with blood streaks in a piglet under three weeks.
items.cause: Likely E. coli or rotavirus; this is a vet call.
items.fix: Get the vet out today. Give oral rehydration solution to the piglet at body temperature. Keep the piglet warm; a chilled scouring piglet rarely makes it through the night.

items.symptom: Whole group scouring after stock arrived from a different holding three days ago.
items.cause: Stress scour from transport and mixing, or a low-grade bug brought in with the new pigs.
items.fix: Watch for blood, mucus, or fever. Call the vet if any of those appear. If the group settles and the scour clears within a week, it was stress. A holding standstill rule was set up for exactly this risk; in future do not introduce new pigs without a quarantine period and a faecal check.

items.symptom: A pig refusing feed, hot to the touch, with scour and visible blood.
items.fix: Call the vet now. Isolate the pig from the group if possible. Provide water and shelter. Do not move the pig further than you have to until the vet arrives.

content.text: Daily check rounds catch scour at the early stage. A dropping check while topping up water adds nothing to the morning routine and saves the vet call when the cause is simple. A scouring pig that looks bright and is still eating can usually be managed at home for two days; one that goes off feed, drops her head, or bleeds in the dropping has crossed into vet territory and waiting another day is the wrong call.
